summary: DHH provides a real-time incident report on Basecamp 3's nearly five-hour outage caused by an integer overflow in a database tracking table. The column was configured as a regular integer (max ~2.1 billion) instead of a big integer, and when IDs were exhausted, the system was forced into read-only mode. The fix required a database migration across 8 databases in two datacenters, followed by careful verification. DHH candidly admits the root cause was a known issue that Rails itself had fixed two years prior by changing its default to big integer, making it an avoidable failure. He takes full personal responsibility and promises a detailed postmortem.
key_points: An integer column overflow at 2,147,483,647 forced Basecamp 3 into read-only mode for nearly five hours
The fix required migrating a very large production table from integer to big integer across 8 databases in two datacenters
Rails, which DHH's team created, had already changed its default from integer to big integer two years earlier
DHH takes full personal responsibility for not heeding a known issue in their own framework
The outage was their worst in roughly a decade, against a normal uptime of 99.998%
money_quotes: That's the most catastrophic failure we've had a Basecamp in maybe as much as decade, and we could not be more sorry.
We've let you down on an avoidable issue that we should have been on top of.
The integer runs out of numbers at 2147483647. The big integer can grow until 9223372036854775807.
Changing a production database is serious business, so we had to test this fix on a staging database to make sure it was safe.
We need to be absolutely certain that all the data is in proper sync before we can go back online. It's looking good, but 99% sure isn't good enough. Need 100%.
It's embarrassing to admit, but the root cause of this issue with running out of integers has been a known problem in our technical community.
We use the development framework Rails (which we created!), and the default setting for that framework move from integer to big integer two years ago.
I accept full responsibility for failing to heed that warning, and by extension for causing the multi-hour outage today.
key_insight: Even the creators of a framework can be bitten by not adopting their own improvements, and the right response is radical transparency and personal accountability.
